coredata.dc:title: Studies on novel BiyXz-TiO2/SrTiO3 composites: Surface properties and visible light-driven photoactivity
coredata.dc:creator: Marchelek, Martyna
Grabowska, Ewelina
Klimczuk, Tomasz
Lisowski, Wojciech
Giamello, Elio
Zaleska-Medynska, Adriana
coredata.dc:description: A series of novel BiyXz-TiO2/SrTiO3 composites were prepared by multistep synthesis route. The as-prepared photocatalysts were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), Raman spectra and BET analysis. The photocatalytic activity test was performed in aqueous solution of phenol under the irradiation of visible light range (λ ≥ 420 nm). Obtained results revealed that the BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 sample exhibit the highest photocatalytic activity under visible irradiation (0.6 μmol/dm3/min). Thus, it was demonstrated that modification of the TiO2/SrTiO3 microspheres by flowers-like structure made of bismuth oxyiodide resulted in enhancement of photocatalytic activity under visible light. The role of active species during the decomposition process of organic compound was investigated using different types of active species scavengers as well as electron paramagnetic resonance analysis (EPR). The study showed that in the BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3/Vis system the holes (h+) plays relevant role in phenol decomposition. Furthermore, the stability and recyclable properties of obtained BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 sample were confirmed during three consecutive processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2/SrTiO3
Bismuth based semiconductors
Photocatalysis
Ternary composites

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of single bismuth consist semiconductors and ternary composites with TiO2/SrTiO3 microspheres
Characterization of single semiconductors and composites
The electron paramagnetic resonance measurement and active species trapping process
Physicochemical properties of the single semiconductors and the composites
Raman and FTIR investigation of lattice vibration modes
Photocatalytic activity in phenol decomposition and specific surface area of obtained photocatalysts
Mechanism of Vis-induced photocatalytic activity of BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3composite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: LeBail refinements of the BiOI (upper panel) and BiOI composite with TiO2 and SrTiO3 (lower panel). The experimental data are represented by open circles, the solid lines represent the fitted pattern and the vertical bars show expected Bragg reflections for BiOI (black), TiO2 (red) and SrTiO3 (green). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Thediffusion reflection spectra of (A) single semiconductors and TiO2/SrTiO3 composite and (B) ternary composites with bismuth consisting semiconductors.
Effect of different scavengers on the degradation of phenol in the presence of (A): TiO2/SrTiO3 and (B): BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3photocatalysts under visible light.
EPR spectra of (A) the TiO2/SrTiO3 sample and (B) the BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 ternary composite.
The high-resolution Bi 4f XPS spectra of (A) pure BiOI, (B) BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 sample before photocatalytic treatment, and (C) BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 after photocatalytic process.
Characteristics of bismuth consisting semiconductors and their composites with TiO2/SrTiO3 microspheres.

row.entry: Phenol degradation rate under visible light, λ > 420 nm [μmol/dm3/min]

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Elemental contents and chemical character of Ti, Sr and Bi atoms in surface layer of pure BiOI and BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3_1 (sample before photocatalytic reaction) and BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3_2 (sample after photocatalytic reaction).

head.ce:title: Studies on novel BiyXz-TiO2/SrTiO3 composites: Surface properties and visible light-driven photoactivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Technology, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Gdansk, Wita Stwosza 63, 80-952 Gdansk, Poland

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Solid State Physics, Faculty of Applied Physics and Mathematics, Gdansk University of Technology, G. Narutowicza 11/12, 80-233 Gdansk, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Applied Physics and Mathematics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physical Chemistry, Polish Academy of Sciences, Kasprzaka 44/52, 01-224 Warsaw, Poland

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Turin, Via P. Giuria 7, I-10125 Torino, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of novel BiyXz-TiO2/SrTiO3 composites were prepared by multistep synthesis route. The as-prepared photocatalysts were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), Raman spectra and BET analysis. The photocatalytic activity test was performed in aqueous solution of phenol under the irradiation of visible light range (λ ≥ 420 nm). Obtained results revealed that the BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 sample exhibit the highest photocatalytic activity under visible irradiation (0.6 μmol/dm3/min). Thus, it was demonstrated that modification of the TiO2/SrTiO3 microspheres by flowers-like structure made of bismuth oxyiodide resulted in enhancement of photocatalytic activity under visible light. The role of active species during the decomposition process of organic compound was investigated using different types of active species scavengers as well as electron paramagnetic resonance analysis (EPR). The study showed that in the BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3/Vis system the holes (h+) plays relevant role in phenol decomposition. Furthermore, the stability and recyclable properties of obtained BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 sample were confirmed during three consecutive processes.

ce:section.ce:para: Heterogeneous photocatalysis has been regarded as one of the effective way to remove the pollutants from the environment [1–3]. Moreover, semiconductor-based photocatalysis has attracted increasing interests due to their potential applications in solar energy conversion, such as hydrogen evolution or artificial photosynthesis [4–6]. Major of the photocatalysts only responds to UV light, constituting only about 4% of the solar beam, due to their relatively large band gaps. Visible light–induced activity of wide band gap semiconductors could be forced by doping or surface modification using metal or non-metal elements [4,7,8]. An alternative route to shift light response of large band gap semiconductors to visible region, is coupling with narrow band gap semiconductors [9].These multicomponent materials could improve the efficiency of a photocatalytic system because of novel or enhanced properties that do not exist in individual components [10].

The titanium dioxide and strontium titanate semiconductors are commonly used in photocatalytic systems because of their high activity, stability and nontoxic properties [11–15]. Moreover, it was found that coupling of n-type TiO2 with p-type SrTiO3 results in enhancement of photogenerated charge carriers (e−/h+) separation and finally in higher photocatalytic activity [13,16–20]. Nevertheless, due to their wide band gaps the utilization of that material is limited to reactions carried out under ultraviolet irradiation. Thus, merging of TiO2 or SrTiO3 with narrow band gap semiconductors should enhanced the photocatalytic ability under visible light range [21]. Formation of new generation components depends on level of energy bands in three semiconductors based on the Serpone’s theory [1,21]. The process should be effective because of: (i) multi(two)-photon excitation of photoactive materials with lower energy photons, (ii) utilization of heterojunctions to drive electronic processes in the desired direction, and (iii) selective photoexcitation of localized electronic states to gain better selectivity [1,21].

In recent years bismuth compounds are found as promising candidates for visible light induced photocatalytic processes due to narrow band gap and layered structure [22–31]. These properties give an opportunity to combine bismuth-containing materials with wide band gap semiconductors and create third generation novel composites [21]. The band gap of different bismuth compounds ranges from 1.3–2.8 eV depending on material types [32–38]. The already described in literature nanocomposites BiyXz-TiO2and BiyXz-SrTiO3 exhibit enhanced activities in photocatalytic degradation of pollutants under visible light illumination, which can be assigned to the optical absorption ability of nanocomposites in higher wavelength region [37,39–51].

Thus, based on band gap structure, the new materials composed of BiyXz-TiO2/SrTiO3 (where BiyXz = Bi2O3, Bi2S3, BiOI, BiOBr, BiVO4, Bi2WO6, Bi2MoO6) should theoretically exhibit higher photocatalytic activity under UV and visible light. The process of multiphoton excitation occurred in ternary composite system should lead to more efficient charge transfer in the desired direction. Moreover, heterogeneous photocatalysis employing 3D microstructures (such as TiO2/SrTiO3 microspheres) is really attractive due to low density of those structures (in comparison with other photocatalysts) and ease of separation after reaction by filtering (e.g. power consumed methods such as centrifugation can be eliminated).

According to our best knowledge, there are no scientific reports on preparation and properties of TiO2/SrTiO3 matrix combined with bismuth consisting semiconductors. The prepared manuscript concerns the novel oriented third generation composites to enhance the efficiency of degradation water pollutants under visible light range. Moreover, until now there was no investigation of active species formed during the illumination in photocatalytic aqueous system based on the ternary structures. Therefore, in the present paper seven different ternary composites were synthesized using solvothermal preparation of TiO2/SrTiO3 followed by formation of bismuth-based semiconductor. In all complex materials the TiO2/SrTiO3 (already investigated in our previous study [52]) plays as the matrix. Furthermore, the composition of ternary materials and the role of different semiconductor in composite have been investigated. The photocatalytic activity of as-prepared nanomaterials was studied in model reaction of phenol degradation in the aqueous phase using visible irradiation source (λ > 420 nm). All as-prepared photocatalysts were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray powder diffraction analysis (XRD), UV–vis diffuse-reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and electron paramagnetic resonance EPR analysis.

All chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. The Titanium (IV) butoxide (TBT) (97%, Sigma-Aldrich) and strontium chloride hexahydrate (SrCl2•6H2O, 99%) purchased from Sigma-Aldrich were used for the preparation of spherical TiO2 and SrTiO3. TheEthanol (99.8%), NaOH and H2SO3 (98%) (POCH S.A., Poland) were used in the synthesis of photocatalytic matrix. The bismuth nitrate (98%, Sigma-Aldrich) was used as a precursor of bismuth in each synthesis of bismuth consisting semiconductors. The Na2WO4 (99%), Na2VO4 (99%), Na2MoO4 (99%), KI (99%), hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 99%), were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich and used depend on the type of prepared semiconductor. Additionally, some reagents like polivinylopirolidon (PVP, 99%) and polyethylene glycol (PEG. 99%) were used as a structuring agent. The tert-Butyl alcohol (99.5%) (TBA), AgNO3 (99%), p-benzoquinone(98%) (BQ) and ammonium oxalate monohydrate (99.5%) from Sigma–Aldrich were used as a scavengers.

Firstly, the TiO2 microspheres were prepared via hydrothermal method described in our previously study [53]. In the typical procedure the 5.1 g of titanium (IV) butoxide was dissolved in 75 ml of absolute ethanol, than 0.33 ml of sulfuric acid (98%) was added. After 10 min of mixing, the 0.30 ml of distilled water was added dropwise to above solution. The mixture was transferred to the Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 4 h. After all, the obtained suspension was washed with distilled water and ethanol several times. At the end the powder was dried in the dryer in 45 °C for 5 h. In the next step the TiO2/SrTiO3 composite was synthesized by alkali hydrothermal methods described by Zheng et al [54]. The 0.5 g anatase TiO2 microspheres prepared by the synthesis above and 1.67 g strontium chloride hexahydrate were mixed with 50 ml of deionized water. The mixture was purged with nitrogen for about 5 min. Then 2 g NaOH was added to the solutions and mixed for 10 min. The hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C in a Teflon autoclave for 45 min was applied to obtain the photocatalyst. The sample was collected and then washed with ethanol and deionized water thoroughly, and finally dried in an dryer at 45 °C [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of single bismuth consist semiconductors and ternary composites with TiO2/SrTiO3 microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation procedures of single bismuth consisting semiconductors and their composites with TiO2/SrTiO3 matrix are presented in Table S1. The weight ratio of bismuth consist materials to the matrix where fixed 1:2 in all ternary composites. All obtained suspensions were washed with water and ethanol several times and dried in 45 °C. The structure of TiO2 and SrTiO3 (1:2 wt.%) in ternary composite with bismuth consist semiconductors is label as TiO2/SrTiO3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of single semiconductors and composites
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal composition of obtained samples was checked by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) technique using X’Pert Pro MPD Philips diffractometer, with Cu Kα radiation λ = 1.5418 Å.

Characterization of atomic composition was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS spectra were recorded on PHI 5000 VersaProbe (ULVAC-PHI) spectrometer with monochromatic Al Kα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV) from an X-ray source operating at 100 μm spot size, 25 W and 15 kV. The high-resolution (HR) XPS spectra were collected with the hemispherical analyzer at the pass energy of 23.5 eV, the energy step size of 0.1 eV and the photoelectron take off angle 45° with respect to the surface plane. The CasaXPS software (version 2.3.16) was used to evaluate the XPS data. Deconvolution of HR XPS spectra were performed using a Shirley background and a Gaussian peak shape with 30% Lorentzian character. The binding energy (BE) scale of all detected HR spectra were calibrated by centering the aliphatic carbon peak (C-C) at 284.8 eV.

The morphology of the photocatalysts was investigated using scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 3D FEG, FEI) working in high vacuum mode as well as transmission electron microscope (TEM-EDX, Tecnai F20 X-Twin, FEI and EDAX spectrometer) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED).
The absorption properties of obtained samples were measured using Evolution 220, Thermo Scientific spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere and BaSO4 was used as the reference.

The Raman spectra were measured with a Perkin-Elmer RamanMicro 200 spectrometer with 785 nm laser at the excitation source under ambient conditions. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer FT-IR Spectrum 2000 spectrometer in the range of 4000–400 cm−1.

To investigate the specific surface area the BET measurement was performed (physical adsorption and desorption of nitrogen at 77 K). The analysis was carried out using the Micromeritics Gemini V200 Shimadzu instrument equipped in the VacPrep 061 Degasser.

The phenol degradation was measured colorimetric method (λmax = 480 nm) using UV–vis spectrophotometer (Evolution 220, Thermo-Scientific). After 30 min of equilibrium, the 0.125 g of photocatalyst suspended in 25 ml of a phenol solution (with concentration 0.21 mmol/dm3) was irradiated with 1000 W Xenon lamp in a cylindrical reactor with quartz window and aeration flow 5 dm3/h. The whole system was cooled with water bath to 10 ± 0.5 °C. The visible light range was adjusted by optical filter with λ ≥ 420 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The electron paramagnetic resonance measurement and active species trapping process
ce:section.ce:para: Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were run using a X-band CW-EPR Bruker EMX spectrometer equipped with a cylindrical cavity operating at 100 kHz field modulation at 77 K. The installation was equipped with cells that can be connected to a conventional high-vacuum apparatus (residual pressure < 10−6 kPa). A microwave power of 1 mW, a modulation amplitude of 0.2 mT and a modulation frequency of 100 kHz were used. The effect of irradiation on EPR spectra was evaluated directly by UV–vis and visible light illumination of the sample into the EPR cavity using a 1000 W Xenon lamp (Oriel instruments) equipped with a IR water filter and the quartz cut off filter with λ ≥ 420 nm.

To clarify which active species are involved in Vis-induced photodegradation of phenol, phenol irradiation process was preceded in the presence of different radicals scavengers. The ammonium oxalate was introduced to the solution of 21 mmol of phenol as scavenger for photogenerated holes, the AgNO3 as scavenger for electrons, the benzoquinone as scavenger for superoxide radical species, and the tert-Butyl alcohol as scavenger for hydroxyl radical species respectively. The concentration of each scavenger obtained in the phenol solution was adjusted to 21 mmol/dm3.The procedure of photocatalytic measurements was the same as in typical degradation process described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of the single semiconductors and the composites
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphology of prepared powders was characterized by scanning and transmission electron microscope. The results of single semiconductors and composites are presented in Figs. 1 and 2
. SEM analysis of pristine bismuth consist photocatalysts confirmed structures obtain according to literature data.

The structure of bismuth oxide formed needles with the length about 10 μm partly covered by some moss layer (Fig. 1A). Similar structures were observed by Wu et al. who used the typical precipitating methods with various amount ratio of bismuth nitrate as precursor and NaOH as precipitating agent [55]. The Bi2O3 was obtained in the form of tubular particles. Previous results showed that the precipitation that occurred at the initial stage of NaOH titration is independent of the formation of Bi2O3 needle-like crystals [55]. Further investigations indicated that due to the higher collision probability, the new Bi2O3 crystals tended to assemble and became crystal bundles or even merged to form rectangular tubes [55]. The Bi2S3photocatalyst was obtained in the form of microflowers formed with rod-like nanostructures (Fig. 1B). Zhu et al. confirmed the tendency of thiourea (Tu) to coordinate the bismuth ions to form Bi3+-(Tu)n complexes, prevents the explosive production of bismuth sulfide and benefits the oriented growth of final nanorods [35]. In consequences the process was described in terms of the following reaction:
Bi3+ + n Tu → [Bi(Tu)n]3+ → Bi2S3nanoflowers [35]

The SEM analysis of BiOI semiconductor showed microspherical structures which are promoted by reaction at pH = 7 (Fig. 1C). Similar to results of Xiao et al. the flower-like microstructures were built from dozens nanopetals with extra smooth surface [56]. The highest magnification images confirmed semitransparent nanostructures ca. 2 μm wide connected to each other through the center and formed desert nanoflowers (see Fig. S1).

During the nucleation process and anisotropic growth the hexagonal BiOBr nanosheets ca. 250 nm in diameter were obtained (Fig. 1D). Unlike to literature data in this case no self-assembly microspheres were observed [57,58]. Other nanosheets with cubic shape were observed for BiVO4 structures obtained during the hydrothermal process (Fig. 1E).The SEM image clearly show that the prepared nanocrystals have a smooth surface. In the literature data many various structures of BiVO4 were obtained and used for degradation of organic compounds [34]. In comparison to BiVO4 the Bi2WO6 photocatalyst was obtained in the form of much smaller nanoplates (Fig. 1F). Xu et al. substituted the ethylene glycol with water and observed formation of bismuth tungstate square plates with a broad grain size distribution [59]. The Bi2MoO6 was prepared in the synthesis at pH = 6 to produce the orthorhombic plate-like nanostructures (Fig. 1G). The same reaction conditions used by Phuruangrat et al. results in the bismuth molybdate with the size ranging from 100 nm to 200 nm and thickness about several nanometers [60].

The SEM images and schematic figures of obtained binary and ternary structure are presented in the Fig. 2. The microscopy analysis confirmed the microspheres morphology of TiO2/SrTiO3 composite structure. The obtained SEM images of pristine TiO2/SrTiO3 shown regular strontium titanate nanocubes on the surface of spherical titanium dioxide. The structure of all samples was confirmed by the XRD analysis. In our previously study it was reported that the surface of pristine TiO2 microspheres was fairly smooth because of the composition with ultrafine nanoparticles [53]. After alkali hydrothermal treatment with SrCl2 the external TiO2 nanocrystals were converted to SrTiO3 semiconductor. Investigation in our previous study with higher magnification SEM analysis reveal that the SrTiO3 are assembled by regular nanocubes with an edge length of 70 ± 10 nm which are piled with each other to form hierarchical microspheres [52]. Moreover, as it was published in our previous study, the HRTEM analysis of TiO2/SrTiO3 powder confirmed two types of lattice fringes: 0.351 nm, and 0.225 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 1) plane of anatase titanium dioxide and (1 1 1) facet of the cubic strontium titanate phase, respectively [52]. Due to this results it was concluded that formation of TiO2/SrTiO3 heterojunction structure by SrTiO3 nanoparticles growing on the surface and side of TiO2 microspheres [52]. Based on our previous results the particle size of the TiO2/SrTiO3had diameters in the range of 4–7 μm, but most of the spheres (about 50%) are within the range of 5–6 μm [52]. The composition of ternary structure build with the TiO2/SrTiO3 and the strictly chosen the bi-consist semiconductor did not modify the morphology of each compound (see Fig. 2).

Fig. 3 presents PXRD patterns for BiOI (upper panel) and BiOI composite with TiO2/SrTiO3 photocatalysts (lower panel). The Le Bail refinements, represented by solid red lines, were conducted by using HighScore software. Estimated lattice parameters for BiOI (P4/nmm) are a = 3.9984(2) Å and c = 9.1647(7) Å in good agreement with values reported by Egbert Keller and Volker Krämer (Z. Naturforsch. 60b, 1255–1263 (2005)). For a composite sample the lattice parameters of BiOI remain almost the same: a = 4.0005(8) Å and c = 9.158(3) Å and two more phases were also refined: TiO2 (I41/amd) with a = 3.3810(3) Å and c = 9.14(2) Å; SrTiO3 (Pm3m) with a = 3.9278(8) Å. The PXRD patterns for all tested samples with detected phases can be found in the Supplementary material (Fig. S2). The observed crystal phases in each samples are listed below in Table 1 .

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the prepared samples are presented in Fig. 4 A and B. It could be noticed that for the bismuth consist semiconductors UV–vis spectra are shifted to infrared light range in comparison to TiO2/SrTiO3. The lowest shift was observed for BiOBr semiconductor and the strongest one for Bi2S3, BiOI and BiVO4. The Bi2S3 (Eg ≈ 1.3 eV) has strong absorption in the whole range of visible and infrared light. The light absorption ability of the composite with TiO2/SrTiO3 is significantly enhanced. It could be attributed to the narrow band gap and large absorption coefficient of Bi2S3 [61]. The other photocatalyst which absorb the visible light range to about 650 nm is the BiOI, with narrow bang gap 1.8 eV [41]. Liu et al. published the same absorption edge at about 630 nm for bismuth oxyiodide and confirmed typical optical absorbance in the visible light region [62]. On the other hand the BiOBr spectra showed the absorption edge at 430 nm with small adsorption in UV region. Due to the fact that the BiVO4 was obtained in two different phases tetragonal and orthorhombic (see Table 1) the UV–vis spectra is more sharped. The adsorption edge of bismuth vanadate is located about 550 nm in the visible region. Wang et al. obtained the three-layer structure composites of BiVO4-TiO2-BiVO4 and observed a red shift in the BiVO4 absorption edge and the absorption intensities of layered structure composites were remarkably higher than that of pure TiO2 and BiVO4 [63]. Many different composites were already prepared to enhanced the visible adsorption properties of photocatalysts [37,63–67]. Thus, the photogenerated charges from the narrow band gap semiconductors could be easily injected into TiO2/SrTiO3 matrix and the composites could serve as a more effective photocatalysts under visible-light irradiation. Moreover, combination with all bismuth consist photocatalyst result in red shifted diffuse reflectance spectra. The higher absorbance in the visible light range should result in improved photocatalytic activity of as prepared materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman and FTIR investigation of lattice vibration modes
ce:section.ce:para: To detect local distortions of the network and crystallographic defects at the molecular level, micro-Raman analysis have been done for all as-prepared samples (presented in Fig. S3).

Non-modified TiO2/SrTiO3 sample showed major Raman bands at 144, 195, 395, 515, 519 (superimposed with the 515 cm−1 band), and 639 cm−1, which can be attributed to the six Raman-active modes of anatase phase with the symmetries of Eg, Eg, B1g, A1g, B1g, and Eg, respectively [68]. Additionally, the Eg peak is mainly caused by the symmetric stretching vibration of OTiO in TiO2, the B1g peak is caused by the symmetric bending vibration of OTi O, and the A1g peak is caused by the antisymmetric bending vibration of OTi O.

Pure Bi2O3 shows several strong Raman bands at 185, 212, 282, 315, 412, and 449 cm−1 [69]. In the case of Bi2O3_TiO2/SrTiO3 composite Raman only detects the presence of the anatase phase with contributions at ca. 144, 195, 395, 515 and 639 cm−1 and the lack of identifiable signals coming from the bismuth component can be observed. This is not an uncommon situation in BiTi composite samples [70–73]. Gómez-Cerezo et al explained that this comes, at least partially, by the fact that bismuth alters anatase photochemistry from relatively low loadings, a fact complicating the detection and analysis of the component [71,74].

The spectrum of Bi2S3 depicts peaks at 112, 238 and 422 cm−1 accord well with the Bi2S3 Raman bands reported in previous literature (see Fig. S3B) [75,76]. In the case of Bi2S3_TiO2/SrTiO3 Raman bands at 144, 395, 515 and 639 cm−1 can be attributed to the Raman-active modes of anatase phase, while peak at 798 cm−1 is a LO4 Raman mode of SrTiO3.

Fig. S3C shows the Raman spectra of BiOl and BiOI_ TiO2/SrTiO3 photocatalysts. For pure BiOI, the band at 85 and 148 cm−1 is assigned to the Eg internal B I stretching mode [77]. For BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 powder TiO2anatase Raman bands occurred and additionally, the band at about 1000–1074 cm−1 corresponded to strontium carbonate (SrCO3).

Due to the tetragonal structure of P4/nmm space group, the Raman active modes of BiOBr have three kinds with A1g, B1g and Eg. The strong band existing at 100 cm−1 and the band at 158.5 cm−1 could be assigned to the A1g internal BiBr stretching mode and Eg internal BiBr stretching mode, respectively [78]. The sharp peaks confirm the good crystallinity. The Eg and B1g band produced by motion of the oxygen atoms at about 385 cm−1 is very weak, which is not readily noticeable.

The Raman spectra of BiVO4 and BiVO4_TiO2/SrTiO3 photocatalysts showed external modes (rotation/translation) located at near 206 cm−1 and 118 cm−1, symmetric Ag bending mode of δ a (VO4 −3) and the asymmetric Bg bending mode of δ as (VO4 −3) appearing at near 363 and 337 cm−1, respectively [79,80]. Moreover, the characteristic and intense peak at 818 cm−1 is assigned to stretching modes νs (VO), with a weak shoulder at ca. 710 cm−1 which is assigned to ν as (VO). For BiVO4_TiO2/SrTiO3 composite the distinct reduction of the band intensities is observed accordingly and characteristic Raman modes of anatase phase appeared.

Raman spectra of the as-prepared Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6_ TiO2/SrTiO3 are shown in Fig. S3F. The peak at 310 cm−1 is assigned to the translation modes involving simultaneous motions of Bi3+ and WO6 6−. For the Bi2WO6, the peaks in the range from 600–1000 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum are assigned to the stretching of the WO band [81]. In more detail, the peaks at 790–820 cm−1 are attributed to antisymmetric and symmetric Ag modes of the O WO groups. The band at 723 cm−1 is interpreted as an antisymmetric bridging mode associated with the tungstate chain [82–84]. For Bi2WO6_TiO2/SrTiO3powder TiO2 anatase phase showed major Raman bands and additionally, the band found at about 1000–1074 cm−1 corresponded to strontium carbonate (SrCO3). Huang et al. explained that SrCO3 contamination can be noted due to the introduction of airborne CO2, which would dissolve as CO3 2− and react with Sr2+ to form SrCO3 during the pre-treatment and/or the post-treatment [85].

For Bi2MoO6 many characteristic bands can be found: 282, 324, 351, 399, 714, 798 and 844 cm−1 [86]. The presence of modes below 400 cm−1 is attributed to both BiO stretches and lattice modes [87]. The A1g peak at 798 cm−1 is originated from the symmetric stretch of a MoO6 octahedron, while the A1g at 716 and 844 cm−1 showed character of orthorhombic distortions of the MoO6 octahedron in Bi2MoO6 with Aurivillius structure of layered slabs (Bi2O2 2+) connected through the corner sharing of distorted MoO6 octahedral [36,88]. The peak at 796 cm−1 which showed the highest intensity and confirmed the formation of Bi2MoO6.
To investigate the chemical composition and chemical bonding of the composites, FTIR spectra of prepared photocatalysts modified by BiyXz were obtained and are shown in Fig. S4.

For Bi2O3 sample intensive signals around 1394 cm−1 and 846 cm−1 as well as 521 cm−1 appearing in the FTIR spectrum of Bi2O3 refer to the stretching vibration of Bi O bonds. In the case of Bi2S3 (Fig. S4A). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy spectra shows the peaks at 1382 and 1100 cm−1 which can be assigned to the Bi S bond vibrations [100]. The characteristic peak at 1637 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of the hydroxyl group (OH) of water from wet atmosphere [94,95].
In the case of Bi2S3 (Fig. S4B) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy spectra show the peaks at 1382 and 1100 cm−1 which can be assigned to the Bi S bond vibrations [100].

The characteristic absorption peak for pure BiOI at 490 cm−1 is attributed to BiO band stretching vibration in BiOI (Fig. S4C). Additionally, Chen et al explained that this absorption peak could be also assigned to the Bi OTi stretching [97,98]. A peak at 574 cm−1 belongs to symmetrical A2u-type vibration of the BiO bond, while the absorption at 772 cm−1 originates from asymmetrical stretching vibration of the Bi O bond [99].

In the spectrum of the unloaded BiOBr, a very sharp peak at 502 cm−1 is observed (Fig. S4D). This is mainly due to the Bi O chemical stretching vibration mode in the crystal phase of BiOBr [96]. Moreover, peak around 1400 cm−1 corresponds to Bi-Br vibrations.

For pure BiVO4 (Fig. S4E), the absorption band at about 650 cm−1 and the shoulder band at 866 cm−1 are associated with the anti-symmetric ν3 (VO4) and symmetric ν1 (VO4) stretching of VO4 −3, respectively [90]. The absorption bands at 1654 cm−1 and at 3000 cm−1 are ascribed to the bending, and stretching vibration of the absorbed water molecules [91].

FTIR spectra of Bi2WO6 show main characteristic bands at 400–800 cm−1, which were ascribed to the Bi O stretching and W OW bridging stretching modes (Fig. S4F). The strong absorption peaks centered at 550, 730, 1382 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibration of Bi O, W O and W OW bridging stretching modes, respectively [92,93]. The peak at 1640 cm–1 could be attributed to the bending vibration of OH group of adsorbed water.

Fig. S4G shows the FTIR spectra of Bi2MoO6 and Bi2MoO6_TiO2/SrTiO3 samples. For pure Bi2MoO6, the bands located at about 840 and 790 cm−1 can be attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching mode of MoO6 involving vibrations of the apical oxygen atoms [24]. The absorption bands located at 555 and 733 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibration and asymmetric stretching modes of MoO6, respectively.

For TiO2/SrTiO3 a strong FT-IR absorption band in the region of 850–650 cm−1 were ascribed to the strong stretching vibrations of Ti O and Ti OTi bonds [89]. Peak located at 1620 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group on the surface and OH bending of dissociated or molecularly adsorbed water molecules. The band centered at 1401 cm−1 is assigned to bending vibrations of the CH bond in the species linking Ti OTi structural network, while band around 2358 cm−1 is the stretching vibrational peak of CO2 (Fig. S4H).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity in phenol decomposition and specific surface area of obtained photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The model contaminant in photocatalytic degradation process was phenol solution in concentration of 20 mg/dm3. Obtained results after the 60 min of irradiation under the visible light range (λ ≥ 420 nm) are presented in Table 1.

The degradation rate of phenol in aqueous solution irradiated by visible light (λ > 420 nm) in the presence of single semiconductors is higher than that one for the TiO2/SrTiO3 microspheres. The bismuth oxybromide achieved decomposition of 0.45 μmol/dm3/min. Zhang et al. synthesized the BiOBr as a plates and microspheres and observed higher photocatalytic decomposition of Methylene Orange under visible light range (λ ≥ 420 nm) using microspheres (about 60% efficiency) than BiOBr plates (about 20% efficiency) after 1 h of irradiation [101]. It was also observed that the BiOBr photocatalyst is active and stable in degradation of Rhodamine B under the irradiation of monochromatic light where λ = 450 nm (∼85% removal after 60 min) [38]. The bismuth oxide achieved 0.41 μmol/dm3/min phenol degradation rate during 60 min of irradiation. It is known as highly active semiconductor under visible irradiation which decomposes contaminants in aqueous environment [48,102]. Chen et al. degrade the malachite green under simulated solar irradiation for 180 min and observed the efficiency of 86.2% [103]. Moreover, the Bi2O3_TiO2 (with a Bi-Ti atomic ratio of 4:96) heterostructure was the most effective in degradation of bisphenol A under simulated sunlight irradiation [104].

In the case of ternary composites, the highest photocatalytic efficiency was noted for the BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 sample (with the BET specific surface area ca. 25.4 m2/g), where the degradation rate achieved 0.6 μmol/dm3/min. Thus, it was revealed that modification of the TiO2/SrTiO3 microspheres by flowers-like structure made of bismuth oxyiodide resulted in enhancement of photocatalytic activity under visible light. Our data obtained for the BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 composite are consistent with literature reports. Boonprakob et al. obtained the composites made of BiOI and TiO2in various molar ratio. The results showed an enhanced phenol degradation of 6 times higher than that of pure BiOI, pureTiO2and Degussa P25. The highly active sample was 10.0 mol% BiOI/TiO2 nanocomposite which achieved about 68% of phenol degradation under Vis irradiation. The photocatalytic performance was explained by a drastic reduction of electron-hole recombination rate and excellent electron-hole separation efficiency of this material. Moreover, its increased visible light harvesting ability and its efficient electron–hole separation efficiency as observed from DRS and photoluminescence spectra results [105]. On the other hand BiOI-TiO2 composite was used in the photocatalytic water splitting process [106]. The structure allows to obtain the photocurrent density of 2.97 mA cm−2 for oxygen evolution. The significant enhancement of performance is ascribed to the enhanced incident photon to current conversion efficiency in the visible region and the reduced electrons and holes recombination [106].

The BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) analysis provided precise specific surface area evaluation of TiO2/SrTiO3 matrix and the ternary composites (see Tab. S1). The TiO2/SrTiO3 sample showed ca. 45.9 m2/g BET surface area. The all composites except the Bi2MoO6_TiO2/SrTiO3 (47.3 m2/g) exhibit lowest specific surface area then the TiO2/SrTiO3. It could be assumed that the combination of single Bi-consist semiconductors with low BET surface area with the TiO2/SrTiO3 matrix results in lower surface area for the ternary composites [32,35,107–109]. It could indicate, that well developed surface area of TiO2/SrTiO3 spheres is partially covered by BiyXz compounds, what is also confirmed by SEM analysis (Fig. 2). Furthermore, the Bi2MoO6_TiO2/SrTiO3 sample achieved only 0.21 μmol/dm3/min degradation rate during the photocatalytic phenol decomposition experiment. The lowest surface area (ca. 2.1 m2/g) was observed in the case of Bi2O3_TiO2/SrTiO3 photocatalyst which archived one of the lowest degradation efficiency. On the other hand, the photocatalytic activity of composites with Bi2WO6 (with BET surface area ca. 30.3 m2/g) was almost unnoticeable. The high degradation rate observed for the BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 indicate that the BET surface area factor is not the most important parameter in utilization of ternary composite for organic pollutants degradation.

To investigate the stability of as-prepared photocatalysts, phenol degradation rate was evaluated in three subsequent cycles in the presence of the most active BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3sample (see Fig. S5). In each process the parameters of reaction was kept constant. Furthermore, no significant decrease was noted during this experiment. Thus, the obtained results confirmed recyclable properties of obtained samples.

On the other hand, combination of titanium dioxide and strontium titanate matrix with other bismuth consist semiconductors results in lower photocatalytic activity under visible light than for pure single photocatalysts. The degradation reaction using ternary composites with Bi2O3 and Bi2WO6 was almost inhibited. It could be assumed that for those composites the multiphoton excitation process was hindered. What is more, it can be suppose that due to ineffective utilization of heterojunctions the electronic processes couldn’t proceed in the desired direction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of Vis-induced photocatalytic activity of BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3composite
ce:section.ce:para: The role of the generated reactive species in the photocatalytic process under visible irradiation was investigate using t-BuOH,AgNO3, p-benzoquinone and ammonium oxalate as the scavengers of ●OH, e−, O2 −● and h+, respectively. The Fig. 5 A and B presented the effect of various additives on the degradation of phenol in the aqueous phase in the presence of BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 and TiO2/SrTiO3(reference sample). In the case of both photocatalysts, usage of ammonium oxalate as the trapping agent of holes resulted in the significantly decline decomposition process of phenol under visible irradiation. As shown in Fig. 5A, no influence of the other scavengers (t-BuOH, AgNO3, p-benzoquinone) was observed during phenol degradation in the presence of TiO2/SrTiO3 suspension. It indicated that, neither the ●OH and e− neither O2 -● took a part in the photocatalytic reaction with organic contaminant.

The same experiments carried out in the presence of BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 clearly shown that the addition of AO had the main influence on photocatalytic efficiency, which were three times lower in comparison to experiments without scavengers. Thus, it demonstrates that the direct oxidation by holes contributed to the observed photocatalytic degradation process of phenol. Furthermore, the addition of TBA and BQ to the solution also reduce photoactivity by a 50 and 25% respectively (see Fig. 5B.). It means, that in the BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3/Vis system, phenols is degraded by h+ but also partly decomposed by O2 −● and ●OH species. In the reaction of holes with hydroxyl anions some hydroxyl radical could be produced. This active species have one of the strong oxidizing potential of 2.8 V (vs NHE) [110]. Moreover, when the e- scavenger (AgNO3) was introduced into the reaction solution the efficiency of photocatalytic process was increased. In literature data was explained that the addition of silver nitrate enhanced the separation of electrons and holes. In consequences, more photogenerated holes and the following active species were produced, which promoted the degradation rate [111]. Obtained results clearly suggested that the photocatalytic elimination of phenol under visible irradiation in the presence of BiOI-TiO2/SrTiO3 semiconductor was intimate with the photogenerated holes. The h+ were the major oxidation species in the process with pure matrix of titanium dioxide with strontium titanate and also when the TiO2/SrTiO3 was modified with bismuth consisting semiconductor. Wang et al. published that the degradation efficiency of methyl orange (MO) on BiOI decreased significantly from 27% to 0% when triethanolamine (TEOA) as a h+ scavenger was added into the photocatalytic reaction solution, confirming photogenerated holes is mainly responsible for MO photodegradation [107].

EPR measurements have been conducted to further convince the above mentioned reaction radicals. The data obtained by electron paramagnetic resonance technique confirmed the results obtained during the photocatalytic degradation test with scavengers. Fig. 6 shows EPR spectra the TiO2/SrTiO3 (see Fig. 6A) and the BiOI_TiO2/SrTiO3 (see Fig. 6B) photocatalysts. The samples were firstly out gassed and the first EPR analysis was conducted (see the black spectra). Further, both powders were irradiated5 s with UV and visible (λ ≥ 420 nm) light, respectively (see red and blue spectra). After that, the photocatalysts were gassed with pure oxygen and also irradiated with two light wavelengths during 5 min. It could be noticed that the introduction of O2 does not affect formation of superoxide radicals. Furthermore, the results obtained during the photocatalytic test with scavengers confirmed this data from EPR analysis. The absence of O2 −● in the EPR test also evident the main role of h+ in the photocatalytic degradation system of organic compounds.

In the present study, we developed successfully various procedures to obtained new 3D ternary composites. Presented results showed that different composites of BiyXz type semiconductors with the TiO2/SrTiO3 matrix exhibit novel photocatalytic and optical properties depend on the bismuth consisting semiconductor used to construct the heterostructure.

In the case of single semiconductors the highest photocatalytic efficiency was observed for BiOBr and Bi2O3 photocatalysts, where the phenol degradation rate reached 0.45 and 0.41 μmol/dm3/min under the Vis irradiation (λ > 420 nm). Moreover, only combining of TiO2/SrTiO3 microspheres with BiOI microstructure results in composite showing significantly higher activity in phenol degradation process under visible light (degradation rate 0.6 μmol/dm3/min). Observed photoactivity is much higher than activity of TiO2/SrTiO3 spheres and pristine BiOI semiconductor (0.13 and 0.20 μmol/dm3/min, respectively). The further investigation indicate that the BET specific surface area parameter has lowest influence on the photocatalytic activity of as prepared ternary composite than the produced heterojunction between the semiconductor structures. Thus, the observed photocatalytic activity of the BiOI-TiO2/SrTiO3 composite must results from interaction between three semiconductors and this study revealed for the first time that composite made with BiOI (Eg ≈ 1.8 eV) seated at the surface of TiO2/SrTiO3 microspheres could be efficiently used to degrade pollutants under solar light irradiation. The formation of heterojunction in the ternary structure could increase the efficiency of charge transfer and prevents the recombination process. The utilization of narrow band gap semiconductor to create the composite results in the extend absorption in the visible light region and improved photocatalytic abilities of proposed structures. The detailed investigation showed that the nanomaterial is stable and recyclable. The experiments with scavengers and EPR analysis proved that the holes were the most active species which took a part in the phenol decomposition reaction. Further investigation should be performed to check the influence of different molar ratio of BiOI to TiO2/SrTiO3 matrix on the photocatalytic performance of those composites under visible light.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Science Centre Poland (contract No.: UMO-2014/15/N/ST8/03753; “Photoexcitation mechanism and surface properties of new 3D BiyXz_TiO2@SrTiO3 composite structure”; PRELUDIUM8 Program).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.11.139

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
